challengeName: Linked Lists, Stacks, Queues, Trees & Heaps

questions.question: What is a Linked List?
options: A linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes, each containing data and a reference to the next node
A contiguous block of memory storing elements
questions.explanation: A Linked List consists of nodes where each node contains data and a pointer/reference to the next node. Unlike arrays, elements are not stored in contiguous memory.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting at the beginning of a Singly Linked List?
questions.explanation: Inserting at the beginning only requires updating the head pointer to the new node, which takes constant O(1) time.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of searching for an element in a Singly Linked List?
questions.explanation: Linked Lists don't support random access. You must traverse from the head node by node until you find the element: O(n) in the worst case.

questions.question: What is a Doubly Linked List?
options: A linked list where each node has pointers to both the next and previous nodes
A list that can only store two types of data
A list with double the capacity
questions.explanation: In a Doubly Linked List, each node contains data, a next pointer, and a previous pointer, allowing traversal in both directions.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of a Doubly Linked List over a Singly Linked List?
options: It allows traversal in both directions and easier deletion of nodes
It stores more data per node
questions.explanation: Doubly Linked Lists allow backward traversal and make deletion easier since you have access to the previous node without extra traversal.

questions.question: What is the space complexity difference between Singly and Doubly Linked Lists?
options: Doubly Linked List uses more memory per node due to the extra previous pointer
Singly Linked List uses more memory
They use the same memory but Doubly is faster
questions.explanation: Each node in a Doubly Linked List stores an extra pointer (previous), consuming more memory per node compared to a Singly Linked List.

questions.question: How do you detect a cycle in a Linked List?
options: Use Floyd's Cycle-Finding Algorithm (Tortoise and Hare) with two pointers moving at different speeds
questions.explanation: Floyd's Algorithm uses a slow pointer (tortoise) and fast pointer (hare). If they meet, there's a cycle. This runs in O(n) time with O(1) space.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm?
questions.explanation: The fast pointer moves at most 2n steps before either finding a cycle or reaching the end. Time complexity is O(n) with O(1) extra space.

questions.question: What is a Stack data structure?
questions.explanation: A Stack follows LIFO — the last element added is the first one removed. Think of a stack of plates.

questions.question: What are the main operations of a Stack?
questions.explanation: The two fundamental stack operations are push (add to top) and pop (remove from top). peek/top is also common to view the top element.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of push and pop operations in a Stack?
questions.explanation: Both push and pop operate at the top of the stack, requiring only a single pointer update: O(1) time.

questions.question: Which problem is Stacks famously used to solve?
options: Checking balanced parentheses in an expression
questions.explanation: Stacks are ideal for checking balanced parentheses — push opening brackets, pop when matching closing bracket is found.

questions.question: What is a Queue data structure?
questions.explanation: A Queue follows FIFO — the first element added is the first one removed. Think of a line of people.

questions.question: What are the main operations of a Queue?
questions.explanation: The fundamental queue operations are enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front).

questions.question: What is a Circular Queue?
options: A queue that only stores circles
A queue where the last position is connected back to the first, efficiently using fixed-size memory
A queue with circular linked lists only
questions.explanation: Circular Queue reuses empty spaces at the front when elements are dequeued. The rear wraps around to the beginning when it reaches the end of the array.

questions.question: What is a Deque (Double-Ended Queue)?
options: A queue that allows insertion and deletion at both ends
questions.explanation: Deque supports O(1) insertion and deletion at both the front and rear ends, making it more flexible than standard queues.

questions.question: What is a Binary Tree?
options: A tree where each node has exactly two children
A tree with two root nodes
A tree where each node has at most two children
A tree where every level is full
questions.explanation: In a Binary Tree, each node can have at most two children: a left child and a right child.

questions.question: What is a Binary Search Tree (BST)?
options: A tree with sorted array backing
A binary tree where left subtree values < node < right subtree values
questions.explanation: BST property: all values in the left subtree are less than the node, and all values in the right subtree are greater. This enables efficient search.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of searching in a balanced BST?
questions.explanation: In a balanced BST, each comparison eliminates half of the remaining nodes, similar to binary search: O(log n).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of searching in an unbalanced BST (worst case)?
questions.explanation: In the worst case (e.g., inserting sorted data), a BST becomes a linked list, and search degrades to O(n).

questions.question: What is the height of a tree?
options: The number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf
questions.explanation: Tree height is the maximum number of edges from the root to any leaf. A tree with just a root node has height 0.

questions.question: What is the depth of a node in a tree?
options: The number of edges from the root to that node
questions.explanation: Depth of a node is the number of edges from the root to that node. The root has depth 0.

questions.question: What is In-order Traversal of a Binary Tree?
questions.explanation: In-order traversal visits: Left subtree first, then Root, then Right subtree. For a BST, this yields values in sorted order.

questions.question: What is Pre-order Traversal of a Binary Tree?
questions.explanation: Pre-order visits: Root first, then Left subtree, then Right subtree. Useful for copying/cloning a tree.

questions.question: What is Post-order Traversal of a Binary Tree?
questions.explanation: Post-order visits: Left subtree first, then Right subtree, then Root. Useful for deleting a tree (children before parent).

questions.question: What is Level-order Traversal?
options: A traversal that skips every other level
A traversal that only visits leaf nodes
A breadth-first traversal visiting nodes level by level from top to bottom
questions.explanation: Level-order (BFS) traversal visits nodes level by level, left to right, using a queue. It visits all nodes at depth d before depth d+1.

questions.question: What data structure is typically used for Level-order Traversal?
questions.explanation: Level-order traversal uses a Queue. The root is enqueued first, then each node's children are enqueued as the node is processed.

questions.question: What is a Complete Binary Tree?
options: A tree where every node has two children
A tree with all leaf nodes at different levels
A tree that is perfectly balanced
A tree where all levels are completely filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right
questions.explanation: Complete Binary Trees have all levels fully filled except the last, and nodes in the last level are as far left as possible. Heaps are complete binary trees.

questions.question: What is a Perfect Binary Tree?
options: A tree where all internal nodes have two children and all leaves are at the same level
A tree with the maximum possible height
A tree where every node has exactly one child
questions.explanation: A Perfect Binary Tree has all internal nodes with exactly two children, and all leaf nodes at the same depth/level.

questions.question: What is a Balanced Binary Tree?
options: A tree where every node has the same number of children
A tree sorted in ascending order
A tree with equal number of left and right nodes
A tree where the height of the two subtrees of every node differs by at most 1
questions.explanation: In a balanced tree, the height difference between left and right subtrees of any node is at most 1. Examples: AVL trees, Red-Black trees.

questions.question: What is a Heap data structure?
options: A complete binary tree where parent nodes satisfy the heap property (min-heap or max-heap)
questions.explanation: A Heap is a complete binary tree. In a max-heap, parent >= children. In a min-heap, parent <= children.

questions.question: What is a Max-Heap?
options: A heap where the smallest element is at the root
A heap that can only store positive numbers
A heap where the parent node is greater than or equal to its children
questions.explanation: In a Max-Heap, the root contains the maximum value, and every parent node is greater than or equal to its children.

questions.question: What is a Min-Heap?
options: A heap where the largest element is at the root
A heap that can only store negative numbers
A heap where the parent node is less than or equal to its children
questions.explanation: In a Min-Heap, the root contains the minimum value, and every parent node is less than or equal to its children.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting an element into a Heap?
questions.explanation: Inserting into a heap adds the element at the end and 'bubbles up' (heapify-up) to restore the heap property: O(log n).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of extracting the minimum/maximum from a Heap?
questions.explanation: Extraction removes the root, replaces it with the last element, and 'bubbles down' (heapify-down): O(log n).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of building a Heap from an array of n elements?
questions.explanation: Building a heap using bottom-up heapify runs in O(n) time, which is more efficient than inserting n elements one by one (O(n log n)).

questions.question: What is a Priority Queue?
options: A queue that processes elements in FIFO order
A queue that only stores numbers
An abstract data type where each element has a priority, and highest priority elements are served first
questions.explanation: Priority Queue serves elements based on priority rather than arrival order. It's commonly implemented using a heap for O(log n) insertions and extractions.

questions.question: How can you find the k-th largest element in an array using a Min-Heap?
options: Sort the array and pick the k-th element
Maintain a Min-Heap of size k, and iterate through the array
Use a Max-Heap of size n
questions.explanation: Maintain a Min-Heap of size k. For each element, if it's larger than the heap's minimum, replace the minimum. The root will be the k-th largest.

questions.question: What is Heap Sort?
options: A comparison-based sorting algorithm that uses a heap structure with O(n log n) time complexity
A sorting algorithm for linked lists
questions.explanation: Heap Sort builds a max-heap, repeatedly extracts the maximum, and places it at the end. It has O(n log n) time and O(1) auxiliary space.

questions.question: Is Heap Sort stable?
options: No, because elements can be swapped far apart during heapify, changing the relative order of equal elements
questions.explanation: Heap Sort is not stable. The heapify process can swap equal elements that are far apart, destroying their original relative order.

questions.question: What is the Next Greater Element problem?
options: For each element, finding the first element to its right that is greater than it
Finding the next element in sorted order
questions.explanation: Next Greater Element for each position is the first element to its right that is larger. It can be solved efficiently using a stack in O(n) time.

questions.question: What data structure is best for implementing the Next Greater Element algorithm?
questions.explanation: A Stack is used to keep track of elements whose next greater element hasn't been found yet. We pop when we find a greater element.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of reversing a Singly Linked List iteratively?
questions.explanation: Reversing a linked list requires visiting each node once and updating pointers. This takes O(n) time with O(1) extra space.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of reversing a Singly Linked List iteratively?
questions.explanation: Iterative reversal uses only a few pointers (prev, current, next) regardless of list size, giving O(1) space complexity.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of reversing a Singly Linked List recursively?
questions.explanation: Recursive reversal uses the call stack with n frames in the worst case, giving O(n) space complexity.

questions.question: What is the diameter of a Binary Tree?
options: The number of nodes on the longest path between any two leaf nodes
questions.explanation: The diameter is the longest path between any two leaf nodes, measured in number of nodes (or edges) on that path.

questions.question: What is a Full Binary Tree?
options: A tree where all levels are filled
A tree where every node has either 0 or 2 children
questions.explanation: In a Full Binary Tree, every node has either 0 children (leaf) or exactly 2 children. No node has only 1 child.

questions.question: What is the Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) in a Binary Tree?
options: The deepest node that has both given nodes as descendants
The first common node in in-order traversal
questions.explanation: LCA is the deepest node that is an ancestor of both given nodes. It can be found by traversing from the root and finding where paths diverge.

questions.question: What is the Mirror/Invert of a Binary Tree?
options: A tree with the same structure
A tree where every left and right child pointer is swapped
A tree sorted in reverse order
questions.explanation: Inverting a binary tree swaps the left and right children of every node recursively or iteratively.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of finding the middle node of a Linked List using the Tortoise and Hare approach?
questions.explanation: The slow pointer moves one step while the fast pointer moves two steps. When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer is at the middle: O(n) time, O(1) space.
